findings.category: A04: Insecure Design / Mobile M4 Insufficient Input/Output Validation
findings.title: Raw inference error message forwarded to UI as Failed(rawCause)
findings.description: ChatViewModel.init maps any failure from registry.initialize(modelName) into ChatUiState.Failed(cause) using e.message verbatim (ChatViewModel.kt:54-59), and ChatScreen.FailedContent renders it as monospace text (ChatScreen.kt:105-110). Per the Task 9 spec and core-runtime code, LlmChatModelHelper.initialize pipes the message through cleanUpMediapipeTaskErrorMessage before it is surfaced, so the documented sanitization happens upstream. There is still no defensive guard in ChatViewModel itself — if any future caller or additional failure path (e.g. an exception thrown inside registry.initialize BEFORE the helper callback, or a failure surfaced from a component other than LlmChatModelHelper) propagates a Throwable whose message contains internal file paths, stack fragments, or native addresses, the UI will display them verbatim. In an offline local-only app this is information disclosure to the local user only, so impact is low, but the ViewModel should not rely solely on an implicit upstream invariant.
findings.impact: If the documented upstream sanitization regresses or a non-mediapipe failure path is added, raw exception text (paths, class names, native error strings) becomes visible in the UI. Attack surface is limited to local shoulder-surfing/support-log scenarios given the offline-only scope.
findings.recommendation: Either (a) defensively re-run the message through a shared sanitizer inside ChatViewModel before constructing Failed, or (b) add a KDoc invariant + unit test asserting that every Throwable surfaced by ModelRegistry.initialize is already sanitized. At minimum, truncate to a known max length and strip control characters to match ErrorLog's sanitize() contract.

findings.title: Fire-and-forget cleanup scope in onCleared() can leak native inference engine memory
findings.description: onCleared() launches registry.cleanup(modelName) on a freshly constructed CoroutineScope(SupervisorJob() + Dispatchers.IO) because viewModelScope is already cancelled (ChatViewModel.kt:133-138). This is intentional per the comment, but the coroutine is unreferenced, unjoined, and unobserved. If the process is killed (Android low-memory killer, user swipe-kill, or a crash in any other coroutine) before cleanup() finishes releasing the native LiteRT-LM engine, the native arena is leaked for the lifetime of that process. Additionally, if cleanup() itself throws, the exception is silently swallowed by the unsupervised scope with no ErrorLog write — violating the Phase-1 error-logging whitelist invariant for `inference-cleanup`.
findings.impact: Native memory can be held until process death; silent failure of cleanup means missing audit trail for the `inference-cleanup` component. Not directly exploitable, but defeats the R3/T9 lifecycle invariant.
findings.recommendation: Wrap the launch body in try/catch (excluding CancellationException) and write failures to ErrorLog with component="inference-cleanup". Consider moving the cleanup-on-clear responsibility to a process-scoped component (application-scope coroutine or a WorkManager OneTimeWorkRequest) so cleanup can survive ViewModel destruction without each VM instantiating its own scope.

findings.title: Unbounded _messages list accumulates in memory with no cap
findings.description: _messages is a MutableStateFlow<List<Message>> that only grows on send() (ChatViewModel.kt:70-73) and is cleared only in reset(). Each streaming token append also allocates a new String via StringBuilder.toString() inside updateLastAssistant (ChatViewModel.kt:89) and a new list copy (ChatViewModel.kt:141-146). A long session with a verbose local model can accumulate tens of MB of strings plus list copies, driving the app toward an OutOfMemoryError. In the Phase-1 ephemeral-history model this is a reliability concern rather than a classic security one, but it is a self-inflicted DoS surface that an attacker with physical device access could trigger deterministically.
findings.impact: OutOfMemoryError crash after prolonged generation; lost in-memory conversation state; on low-RAM devices the risk appears sooner.
findings.recommendation: Either cap the in-memory message count (e.g. keep last N=200 messages plus a 'truncated' marker) or cap total character count of assistant text retained. Also consider updating the streaming message by diff rather than rebuilding the whole list on every token — StateFlow.update + toMutableList on each token is O(N) per token.

findings.title: error() in ViewModel init and send() converts recoverable states into process crashes
findings.description: ChatViewModel throws kotlin.error(...) in two places: (1) ChatViewModel.kt:42 if SavedStateHandle lacks "modelName", and (2) ChatViewModel.kt:68 in send() if registry.getModel(modelName) returns null despite uiState being Ready. Both throw IllegalStateException during normal Android flows (configuration change restoring a VM whose nav args were rewritten; registry state mutated from another screen between the Ready transition and send()). Because these execute on the main thread inside ViewModel construction / UI callbacks, they crash the app. A crashing ViewModel on restore can also create a reboot-loop when the saved nav destination is restored at next launch.
findings.impact: Denial-of-service of the Chat feature via crash loop on malformed nav state. No confidentiality/integrity impact given local-only scope.
findings.recommendation: For the missing nav arg: transition to ChatUiState.Failed with a sanitized message and let FailedContent + onBack take the user home. For the null-model race in send(): also transition to Failed (or log to ErrorLog component="inference" and revert isGenerating flag) instead of crashing.

findings.title: Inference onError log write is fire-and-forget in viewModelScope; may be dropped on teardown
findings.description: onError wraps errorLog.e(...) in viewModelScope.launch (ChatViewModel.kt:106-108). If the user navigates back between the moment the native engine fails and the moment the launch is dispatched, viewModelScope is cancelled and the log write never happens — silently. The Phase-1 error-log channel is the only record of inference failures (ErrorLog.kt) and dropping entries under cancellation breaks the audit trail contract.
findings.impact: Undercounting of inference failures in post-mortem debugging; potential blind spot during security incident investigation.
findings.recommendation: Use an application-scoped dispatcher (e.g. an @ApplicationScope CoroutineScope injected by Hilt) for ErrorLog writes so they are not tied to the lifetime of the originating ViewModel. Alternatively, expose a non-suspend fire-and-forget entry point on ErrorLog that owns its own scope.

findings.category: A01: Broken Access Control / Mobile M10 Extraneous Functionality
findings.title: modelName nav-arg is trusted without allowlist check in the ViewModel
findings.description: The only validation of modelName is the registry.getModel(modelName) == null check after initialize(). If the Task 10 NavHost eventually accepts a deep-link or implicit intent carrying an arbitrary modelName string, ChatViewModel will happily call registry.initialize(modelName) with untrusted input. The actual filesystem/URL resolution happens inside DefaultModelRegistry (not this module), so whether a crafted modelName can traverse to an arbitrary path or URL is a property of that layer — but from this ViewModel's perspective there is no defence-in-depth check (e.g. 'does the allowlist contain this name'). Given Phase-1 is single-process offline with no exported activities for this route (yet to be confirmed in Task 10), impact today is low, but the risk surface expands the moment the chat route becomes reachable via a deep link.
findings.impact: If the chat route is ever exported via a deep link without an allowlist check in NavHost, a malicious app or crafted URL could pass an arbitrary modelName and exercise the full registry.initialize code path on attacker-chosen input.
findings.recommendation: In Task 10 ensure the chat route is NOT exported (android:exported=false on the host Activity, or no <intent-filter> on the deep link). As defence-in-depth, add a fast-path check in ChatViewModel.init: call registry.models.value.any { it.model.name == modelName } before initialize(); if false, transition to Failed with a generic 'model not found' message and log to ErrorLog component="inference-init".
